title: Auth & Security
description: Authentication, token storage, and security best practices
lesson:
Security on Android

Android apps need to handle authentication tokens, sensitive data, and network security carefully. Key principles:

  • Never store tokens in plain SharedPreferences — use EncryptedSharedPreferences
  • Never hardcode API keys — use BuildConfig with local.properties
  • Use certificate pinning for sensitive API communication
  • Apply ProGuard/R8 to obfuscate release builds

Token Storage

// BAD: Plain text, readable on rooted devices SharedPreferences.edit().putString("token", token).apply() // GOOD: Encrypted with Android Keystore EncryptedSharedPreferences.create(...).edit() .putString("token", token).apply()

questions.title: Secure Token Storage
questions.description: Where is the most secure place to store authentication tokens on Android?
options: A text file in internal storage
A static variable in the Application class
questions.explanation: EncryptedSharedPreferences encrypts both keys and values using AES-256. Regular SharedPreferences stores plain XML. Static variables are lost on process death. For highest security, use the Android Keystore for key material.
hints: Regular SharedPreferences files are stored as plain XML

questions.title: Insecure Token Storage
questions.description: This code stores the auth token insecurely. Fix it to use encrypted storage.
questions.explanation: EncryptedSharedPreferences is a drop-in replacement encrypting both keys (AES256_SIV) and values (AES256_GCM). The MasterKey is stored in the Android Keystore (hardware-backed on most devices).
hints: There is a direct encrypted replacement in AndroidX

questions.title: Certificate Pinning
questions.description: What is certificate pinning and why would you implement it?
questions.answer: Certificate pinning validates that the server's certificate matches a known, expected certificate (or public key hash), rather than trusting any CA-signed certificate. This prevents MITM attacks where an attacker uses a rogue CA certificate to intercept HTTPS traffic.
questions.explanation: Normally, Android trusts any certificate signed by a trusted CA. Pinning adds a second check: the certificate must match a specific hash embedded in the app. Tradeoff: if the server's certificate changes, you must update the app.
hints: What if someone installs a rogue CA on the device?

questions.title: Biometric Authentication
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to set up biometric authentication.
questions.explanation: The Biometric API uses a builder for configuration, a callback for results, and authenticate() to show the system dialog. Always check BiometricManager.canAuthenticate() first.
hints: The dialog configuration uses a Builder pattern

questions.title: OAuth2 Flow
questions.description: In the OAuth2 Authorization Code flow for a mobile app, what is the correct order of steps?
options: Get token → Redirect to provider → Exchange code → Use token
Redirect to auth provider → User grants permission → Receive auth code → Exchange code for tokens
Send username/password directly → Receive token → Use token
Generate token locally → Validate with server → Use token
questions.explanation: The Authorization Code flow: 1) Redirect user to the auth provider's login page. 2) User authenticates and grants permission. 3) Provider redirects back to your app with an authorization code. 4) Your app exchanges the code for access and refresh tokens. Mobile apps should use PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) for additional security. Never send credentials directly — that's the Resource Owner Password flow, which is deprecated.
hints: The user authenticates with the provider, not your app directly
An intermediate "code" is exchanged for the final tokens

questions.title: Auth Interceptor
questions.description: Write an OkHttp Interceptor that adds a Bearer token from a TokenManager to every request. If the token is null, proceed without it.

checks.message: Get the original request and proceed with the chain
Add the Authorization header with Bearer token

questions.explanation: The interceptor gets the original request, checks for a token, and creates a new request with the Authorization header if a token exists. chain.proceed() sends the request down the chain. This centralizes auth logic — every API call automatically includes the token.
hints: Build a new request from the original, adding the header
chain.proceed() must always be called to continue the request
